read-tracked-and-untracked-state.label: Read the Repository State
read-tracked-and-untracked-state.summary: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.

sketch2.title: Tracked, Staged, and Committed Are Different

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Recognize an Untracked File
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository.

Use the full status report to identify the new `schedule.md` file before it has ever been staged.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Look for the section where Git lists files that have no recorded history yet.

help.concept: `git status` describes staged, unstaged, and untracked work without changing the repository.
help.hint_1: Read the full report and find the path named in the task.
help.hint_2: The file should appear as untracked because it has never entered the staging area or history.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
cd event-board
printf %s '# Event Schedule
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Recognize a Staged File
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository.

`schedule.md` is already staged for the first snapshot. Use short status and a staged-name listing to verify that it is in the index.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Review the proposed snapshot as a list of paths.

help.concept: A staged file is in the index and will be included in the next commit unless the staging area changes.
help.hint_1: The staging area should contain only files that serve this checkpoint.
help.hint_2: Compare the staged list with the requested commit boundary before checking.

set -eu

git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Recognize a Tracked Modification
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository.

The board has one committed schedule, and its time has changed in the working tree. Use short status and `git diff` to inspect the unstaged edit.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Inspect the working-tree patch before changing the staging area.

help.concept: A tracked modification differs from the last committed snapshot but has not entered the staging area.
help.hint_1: Focus on the removed and added lines for the path named in the task.
help.hint_2: The patch should contain only the edit described in the scenario.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf event-board
mkdir -p event-board
git -C event-board init -q -b main
git -C event-board config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C event-board config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 9 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..
git -C event-board add -A
git -C event-board commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd event-board
printf %s 'Doors open at 10 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..

cd ..
git -C event-board add -A
git -C event-board commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'

printf %s 'Doors open at 10 AM.
' > schedule.md
cd ..

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Confirm a Clean Working Tree
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `event-board/` repository.

The current event-board snapshot is committed and unchanged. Use full and short status to confirm that there is nothing left to stage or commit.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Use the compact repository report and read both status columns.

help.concept: A clean working tree means the files, index, and current commit agree.
help.hint_1: Focus on the path named in the task and decide whether it is untracked, staged, or modified.
help.hint_2: Compare the final status with the exact state the prompt asks you to prove.

' > schedule.md
cd ..

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git status
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git status` command?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To commit changes to the repository
options.b: To show the current branch and changes
options.d: To delete files from the repository

multi-choice-1.title: Components of git status output
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following are components of the `git status` output? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the states a new file passes through during a complete first snapshot.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t4: Clean relative to the new commit

sketch0.title: Let Git Describe the Folder
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
`git status` is the safest first question in an unfamiliar repository. It names the current branch and groups work by where it lives: staged for the next commit, changed only in the working tree, or not tracked yet.

Suppose `README.md` was edited and `schedule.md` is new. The full report keeps those facts separate and explains what can be staged or unstaged. Running it changes nothing.

While you are learning, the full view is valuable because its headings tell a story. Later, the short view gives the same map in a compact form.

For a first inspection, use the full report:

```bash
git status
```

If `draft.md` is new, Git places it under **Untracked files**. The command is read-only: it describes the repository without staging or changing anything.

sketch1.title: Read the Two Short-Status Columns
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Short status compresses the report into two meaningful columns:

```text
?? schedule.md
 M README.md
M  agenda.md
```

`??` means Git has never tracked the path. A mark in the **left** column describes the staging area. A mark in the **right** column describes the working tree. Therefore ` M README.md` is modified but unstaged, while `M  agenda.md` is already staged.

The spaces are part of the notation. Read both columns before deciding what to do next.

The compact report and the staged-name view answer two different questions:

```bash
git status --short
git diff --staged --name-only
```

The first shows every current state in two columns. The second lists only the paths already selected for the next commit. For example, a staged `agenda.md` appears in both views, while an unrelated unstaged file appears only in status.

sketch2.title: Tracked, Staged, and Committed Are Different
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A tracked file has appeared in repository history before. Git can compare its current version with the staged version and the latest commit. An untracked file has no recorded baseline yet.

Staging a new file changes its status from `??` to `A `, but the file is still not part of history. Staging prepares a proposed snapshot; committing records it.

That distinction prevents a common beginner mistake: assuming that because a file was added to the staging area, it has already been saved in history.

When a tracked file has an unstaged edit, narrow the comparison to that path:

```bash
git diff -- notes.md
```

The `--` separates command options from the file name. Git then shows only the working-tree changes in `notes.md`; it does not stage or commit them.

sketch3.title: Inspect, Act, Inspect Again
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
A dependable Git workflow has a quiet rhythm: inspect the state, make one deliberate change, then inspect again.

The second inspection catches the mistakes commands alone cannot: working in the wrong repository, staging an unrelated path, or believing a commit is complete while an edit remains. A clean short-status result is not merely “nothing happened”; it is evidence that the working tree and staging area match the current commit.

When status surprises you, pause. Git is showing the exact state that the next command would act on.

Use both views when you want a detailed explanation and a compact confirmation:

```bash
git status
git status --short
```

A clean repository produces the full “nothing to commit” message and no short-status rows. Reading both makes the quiet short result easier to trust.
